title: Constraint-Market Compatibility: Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions

sections.title: Market Snapshot
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS** | 712110 |
| **Title** | Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions |
| **FP fit** | adjacent |
| **Confidence** | 0.80 |
| **Est. market size** | ~$120M global |
| **Job to be done** | Protect exhibition and storage areas by monitoring emergency exits for unauthorized use while maintaining fire evacuation compliance in heritage-listed buildings that prohibit structural modification |
| **Verdict** | **SURVIVING** — no knockouts |

---

sections.title: Verdict Table (all 10 constraints)
sections.content:
| # | Constraint | Type | is_absolute | Verdict | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | Emergency Escape Non-Impedance | regulatory | false | **none** | Product pre-certified to EN 179/EN 1125; museum fire codes impose identical requirement already met |
| C2 | Mechanical Alarm Independence | regulatory | **true** | **none** | Absolute constraint already satisfied by mechanical-primary design; museum context adds no new power-failure risk |
| C3 | Wireless Range Limitation | physical | false | **mitigable** | Historic stone/masonry walls attenuate 868 MHz beyond standard 30 m; repeaters required in large complexes |
| C4 | IoT Battery Life Floor | operational | false | **none** | Emergency exit door events are infrequent in museums; battery comfortably exceeds 2-year target |
| C5 | Retrofit-Only Surface Mount | physical | false | **mitigable** | Most museums: surface mount preferred (reversible, no structural modification). Grade I / Historic Monument contexts need heritage authority consultation on visible attachment |
| C6 | EasyWave Protocol Lock-In | operational | false | **mitigable** | Museum BMS platforms (Honeywell, Siemens Desigo, Johnson Controls Metasys) use BACnet/IP; GfS BUS gateway bridges protocols |
| C7 | IoT Price Premium | economic | false | **mitigable** | Public institution / nonprofit budgets constrained; heritage security grant funding and guard-patrol ROI case offsets premium |
| C8 | GDPR Monitoring Data Compliance | regulatory | false | **mitigable** | EU museums in scope; legitimate interest (fire safety, heritage protection) provides legal basis; DPA + DPIA for staff-monitoring contexts |
| C9 | IP Protection Boundary | environmental | false | **none** | Museum galleries and collection areas are climate-controlled, dry, indoor environments; IP45 outdoor variant covers any external site access points |
| C10 | EN 13637 / EltVTR Electrical Compliance | regulatory | false | **mitigable** | Applies only if Türwächter combined with powered locking in conservation vault or restricted zone; standalone alarm-only deployment has no exposure |

---

sections.title: Mitigable Constraint Details
sections.content:
### C3 — Wireless Range Limitation

**Rationale:** Heritage museum buildings — stone, brick, reinforced vault structures — attenuate 868 MHz signals significantly. A 30 m effective range can require 3–5 repeater stages across a large gallery wing or underground storage level. RF survey is mandatory pre-installation. Infrastructure cost is low but planning adds lead time.

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Mitigation actions** | 1) Conduct RF coverage survey before installation to map attenuation zones; 2) Deploy wireless repeaters at 20–25 m intervals through masonry structure; 3) Install GfS BUS gateway per wing for centralized aggregation |
| **Cost estimate** | low (<$10K — repeaters ~€50–100 each; typical museum wing: 2–5 units) |
| **Time estimate** | 2–4 weeks |

---

### C5 — Retrofit-Only Surface Mount

**Rationale:** Surface-mount, non-drilling installation is a **positive differentiator** for heritage buildings — it satisfies the "reversible modification" requirement that heritage authorities (English Heritage, Denkmalschutz, DRAC) impose. For the vast majority of museums this is an advantage, not a barrier. The only real risk is aesthetic: RAL 6029 safety green on a historic timber door in a Grade I / Monument Historique context may require heritage authority sign-off.

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Mitigation actions** | 1) Request custom RAL color matching historic door finish (GfS supports custom colors); 2) Apply for Listed Building Consent / Denkmalschutzbehörde approval where surface attachment to listed fabric requires permit; 3) Prioritize back-of-house and service exit installations where aesthetic controls are lighter |
| **Cost estimate** | low (<$10K — heritage consultation €1–3K; no engineering) |
| **Time estimate** | 4–8 weeks (LBC/permit process) |

---

### C6 — EasyWave Protocol Lock-In

**Rationale:** Museum BMS platforms (Honeywell Building Technologies ~30%, Siemens Desigo CC ~25%, Johnson Controls Metasys ~25% per VN data) all use BACnet/IP or Modbus. EasyWave has no native interoperability. GfS BUS gateway outputs potential-free contacts that can be wired to BMS dry-contact inputs — a standard integration path. For institutions with no BMS or running standalone GfS monitoring dashboards, this constraint disappears entirely.

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Mitigation actions** | 1) Deploy GfS Wireless BUS Interface as EasyWave-to-contact bridge; 2) Wire potential-free relay outputs to museum BMS I/O module for event ingestion; 3) For institutions without BMS (<20% of market), use standalone GfS monitoring dashboard — no integration required |
| **Cost estimate** | low (<$10K — BUS gateway ~€200–500; integration labor 1–2 days) |
| **Time estimate** | 1–3 weeks |

---

### C7 — IoT Price Premium

**Rationale:** ~€730/door for IoT variant vs. ~€300–400 for basic mechanical. Museums are typically public institutions or nonprofits operating under constrained capital budgets. A 50-door museum deployment is ~€36,500 IoT vs. ~€15,000–20,000 mechanical — a ~€17,000 gap. However: (1) heritage security grant programs exist in EU, UK, and US specifically for building security upgrades; (2) patrol labor savings are significant — museums employ dedicated security staff for exit monitoring; (3) artifact theft prevention value dwarfs hardware cost.

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Mitigation actions** | 1) Build guard patrol hour reduction ROI model per institution (target: 60–90% reduction in exit-specific patrol labor); 2) Identify applicable heritage security grants (EU Horizon, UK National Lottery Heritage Fund, US IMLS, national programs); 3) Offer volume deployment pricing for multi-wing rollout |
| **Cost estimate** | low (<$10K — grant writing and ROI modeling) |
| **Time estimate** | 3–6 months (grant cycle) |

---

### C8 — GDPR Monitoring Data Compliance

**Rationale:** EU museums in scope for GDPR if door event logs are attributable to identifiable staff (curators, security, maintenance). Visitor use of emergency exits is generally non-attributable. The legal basis for processing is "legitimate interest" (fire safety, heritage protection, theft prevention) — well-established and relatively easy to document. DPIA required if systematic staff monitoring is in scope.

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Mitigation actions** | 1) Document legitimate interest basis in privacy notice covering fire safety and heritage asset protection; 2) Execute DPA with GfS and/or monitoring platform provider; 3) Complete DPIA for institutions conducting systematic monitoring of employee movement through designated exits |
| **Cost estimate** | low (<$10K — legal/DPO consultation €2–5K) |
| **Time estimate** | 4–8 weeks |

---

### C10 — EN 13637 / EltVTR Electrical Compliance

**Rationale:** Applies only when the Türwächter is combined with powered locking (electromagnetic holders, access control readers) in high-security zones — conservation vaults, restricted collection storage. VN data shows museums use IP-based electronic access control from ASSA ABLOY, Dormakaba, and Salto. Combined configurations in these zones must pass EN 13637 fail-safe system design review. Standalone alarm-only deployment (the typical museum scenario) has zero exposure.

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Mitigation actions** | 1) For standalone alarm-only deployment: no action required; 2) For combined configurations with powered locking: verify combined system fail-safe release against EN 13637 requirements at system design stage; 3) Obtain combined system certification before commissioning controlled-access areas |
| **Cost estimate** | low (<$10K — system design review €1–3K; applies to combined-system deployments only) |
| **Time estimate** | 2–4 weeks |

---

sections.title: Key Finding
sections.content:
**No knockouts. Market survives constraint screening.**

The museum/heritage market is uniquely favorable for the Türwächter's constraint profile:

| Advantage | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| C2 (absolute) pre-satisfied | Mechanical-primary design is regulatory requirement — met by architecture |
| C5 (surface mount) flips positive | "No structural modification" is the heritage authority requirement; retrofit surface mount is the compliance answer, not the constraint |
| C3, C4 mitigated by building type | Stone/masonry RF attenuation addressable with repeaters; low door-event frequency means battery life is excellent |
| C9 irrelevant | Climate-controlled dry environments; IP45 outdoor variant covers exterior access points |

The hardest mitigation in this market is C7 (price premium) for budget-constrained public institutions — partially offset by grant pathways — and C3 (range) in large historic stone complexes, both low-cost and well-understood mitigations.

---

sections.title: Aggregate Result
sections.content:
| Output | Value |
|---|---|
| **markets_eliminated** | `[]` — no knockouts |
| **markets_surviving** | `["712110"]` |
| **Highest-cost mitigation** | C7 — IoT Price Premium (budget constraints + grant cycle time) |
| **Stacked mitigation complexity** | Low-medium (C3 + C5 + C6 + C7 + C8 ± C10 — all low-cost, no single blocker) |

---
